{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.5, "R1.2": 0.8, "R1.3": 0.9, "R1.4": 0.5, "R1.5": 0.9, "R1.6": 0.8, "R1.7": 0.7}}

Summary: Reorganized the draft into a standard motivation→method→theory→results→related-work flow, flagged several claim/evidence mismatches (most notably the AlpacaEval-2 phrasing and the tautological-sounding ablation), tightened the formalism, and asked for citations/years that are missing.

**Substantive issues to address before the revision below is final**

1. *AlpacaEval-2 does not directly measure reward hacking.* The current text says "reduces reward hacking on AlpacaEval-2 by 18%." AlpacaEval-2 reports length-controlled win-rate against a reference model; it is not a reward-hacking benchmark. Either (a) change the metric to one that does measure hacking (e.g., reward over-optimization curves à la Gao et al. 2023, or a length/sycophancy diagnostic), or (b) reword to "reduces length-controlled overgeneration on AlpacaEval-2 by 18 points" — but only if that is what the number actually is.

2. *The HH-RLHF timeline is in tension with the motivation.* HH-RLHF was collected in 2022, predominantly before ChatGPT was widely available to crowdworkers. If ρ is meant to be high there, you need evidence; if it is low there, the 7.4-point gain on HH-RLHF needs a different explanation. Consider either reporting C's estimated ρ on HH-RLHF, or restricting the headline claim to the Prolific set.

3. *The ablation sentence reads as tautological.* "Ablating the reweighting term recovers the baseline performance" is by construction true if reweighting is your only intervention. I assume you mean: training a reward model on the same data with C's features available but without using them as weights does not improve over baseline — i.e., the gain is from the weighting, not from leakage of C into the representation. Say that explicitly.

4. *Theorem 1 phrasing is loose.* "Bounded away from the true human-preference rate by at least Ω(ρ²)" mixes a lower bound with asymptotic notation in an unusual way; readers will want "at least cρ² for an explicit constant c depending on [stated quantities]," and the "true human-preference rate" should be defined (population accuracy of the Bayes-optimal reward model on uncontaminated pairs, presumably). State the "mild calibration assumption" inline (one clause), since "mild" without content is a yellow flag.

5. *Classifier inputs are under-specified.* "Lexical entropy" as an *annotator* feature is unusual — entropy of what, across which sample? And C is described as taking (annotator, response), but the weight is applied per preference triple (which has two responses). Specify whether C scores the chosen response, both, or the triple, and what is aggregated.

6. *"Frontier-model outputs" needs a date and model list* — the n-gram overlap feature is only meaningful relative to a specific reference corpus. Given that contamination dynamics change quarterly, list which models' outputs you indexed and at what date.

7. *Missing citations / years.* The 33–46% figure needs a source (Veselovsky et al. 2023? Liang et al. 2024?). Christiano et al., Stiennon et al., Ouyang et al. should carry years. The "contamination-aware learning … for supervised image classification" claim should cite at least one representative work (e.g., Natarajan et al. 2013 for noisy labels, or Patrini et al. 2017 for loss correction) so the novelty delta is auditable.

8. *Novelty claim is too narrow as written.* "Not, to our knowledge, for preference learning over LLM outputs" leaves open whether contamination-aware methods have been applied to *human* preference learning (e.g., noisy preference learning in IRL or bandits). Address that explicitly, since reviewers will ask.

9. *No comparison to obvious baselines.* The natural competitors are (i) hard filtering — drop triples C flags above a threshold; (ii) annotator-level filtering — drop whole annotators; (iii) DPO-style robust losses. Without at least one of these, the contribution reads as "we did the soft version of an obvious idea."

10. *Sample size disclosure.* n = 2,400 triples across 84 annotators is ~29 triples/annotator — small for any annotator-level feature in C. Acknowledge or report a learning curve.

---

**Revised draft**

Recent audits estimate that 33–46% of text-production crowdworkers on MTurk and Prolific routinely use ChatGPT-class assistants to draft their submissions [CITE: Veselovsky et al. 2023; …], and a non-trivial fraction of "human" preference labels collected after late 2022 appear at least partly LLM-generated. This creates a specific failure mode for RLHF: when the model family being aligned also produced the preference labels, the reward model collapses toward a degenerate self-preference signal rather than recovering human values. We address this failure mode directly.

We propose **ContamRLHF**, a two-stage reward-model training procedure that downweights preference triples likely to have been LLM-assisted. First, a contamination classifier C is fit to predict whether a given (annotator, chosen-response, rejected-response) triple was produced with LLM assistance, using annotator metadata (median response time, per-annotator token-distribution entropy across submissions) and per-response n-gram overlap with a reference index of outputs from {GPT-4, Claude-3, Gemini-1.5} sampled in [DATE]. Second, the standard Bradley–Terry preference loss is reweighted by (1 − C(triple)) for each triple, so that high-confidence contaminated examples contribute little to the reward-model gradient.

Formally, let ρ ∈ [0,1] denote the population fraction of preference triples whose chosen label was emitted by an LLM. Theorem 1 (Appendix B) shows that under [explicit assumption on label-flip structure], the standard Bradley–Terry estimator's excess risk against the uncontaminated Bayes-optimal reward is at least cρ² for an explicit constant c > 0 when ρ > 0.3; under the additional assumption that C is calibrated to within ε in TV distance on the contaminated subpopulation, ContamRLHF restores an unbiased estimator up to O(ε).

We evaluate on Anthropic's HH-RLHF [CITE] and on a held-out preference set we collected via Prolific (n = 2,400 triples, 84 annotators, collected [DATE]). C estimates ρ at [X]% on HH-RLHF and [Y]% on the Prolific set. ContamRLHF improves downstream RLHF win-rate by 7.4 points (95% CI [a, b]) over the standard pipeline. On AlpacaEval-2 length-controlled win-rate, it reduces [the specific failure mode you actually measured — name it] by 18 points. As a control against the possibility that C's features merely act as useful side information, we train a reward model that consumes the same C features without using them as weights; this matches the baseline within noise, isolating the gain to the reweighting itself. We additionally compare to two natural alternatives — hard filtering at C > τ, and dropping any annotator whose mean C exceeds τ — and find ContamRLHF outperforms both by [Z] points, attributable to its retention of partially-clean signal from high-ρ annotators.

Prior RLHF pipelines [Christiano et al. 2017; Stiennon et al. 2020; Ouyang et al. 2022] assume clean human labels. Loss-correction methods for label noise are well established in supervised classification [Natarajan et al. 2013; Patrini et al. 2017], and noisy-preference learning has been studied in bandit and IRL settings [CITE]; to our knowledge, ContamRLHF is the first to target *LLM-generated* contamination in preference learning over LLM outputs, where the contaminating distribution is structurally aligned with the model being trained.

---

*Uncertainty disclosure.* The specific citations I inserted as placeholders (Veselovsky et al. 2023; Natarajan et al. 2013; Patrini et al. 2017; Gao et al. 2023) are plausible but unverified for this venue/context — confirm before submission. My characterization of AlpacaEval-2 as a length-controlled win-rate (not a reward-hacking metric) is my main factual claim against the draft; verify against your actual evaluation setup. The Theorem 1 reformulation is a guess at what you proved — replace with the actual statement.
